title: The shape of data: histogram and box plot
intro: Points on a line work while the numbers are few. But the "Slice" chain has grown to fifty employees — individual points no longer tell you anything. You need to see the whole shape: where salaries are dense, where sparse, whether there are outliers. So the data is dropped into "bins" — ranges — counting how many people fall into each. That is a histogram.

beats.text: The height of a bar is how many people earn a salary from that range. The main thing is visible at once: there is a "hump" where most people are (around 33–37k) and thin edges. That is the shape of the data — in a single glance.
Bin width is a choice, and it changes the picture.
beats.predict: Play with the "number of bins". What happens to the shape when there are too few bins, or too many?
beats.reveal: Too few bins — everything merges into a couple of bars and the shape is lost. Too many — nearly every value gets its own bar, and you see noise instead of shape. The truth is in the middle: enough bars to make out the hump and the tails.

beats.text: Below the histogram is a compact summary of the same data, the "box-and-whiskers" (box plot). The line inside the box is the median; the box spans Q1 to Q3, covering the middle half of the people; the whiskers reach the edges of ordinary values.
beats.predict: Now press "add the director" with their big salary. What appears on the right of the histogram and on the box plot?
beats.reveal: On the histogram a lonely bar grows far to the right — a long tail. On the box plot the director's salary flies past the whisker and is marked as a separate dot: an outlier, caught automatically.

beats.text: How does the box plot know what counts as an outlier? A simple rule: whiskers extend no farther than 1.5 box-widths (1.5·IQR) from the box edges, and anything beyond gets its own dot. That is not "an error" — it is a formal signal: "this value is unusually far from the bulk, take a look".

The same box plot reads nicely through percentiles. The median is the 50th percentile (half the people earn less). The bottom of the box, Q1, is the 25th; the top, Q3, the 75th. Service dashboards often watch the high percentiles: p95 and p99 of response time mean "how bad it is for the unluckiest 5% and 1% of users" — and that tail usually matters more than the mean.

One picture answers everything at once: where the center is (median), how wide the spread is (box and whiskers), whether there are outliers (dots past the whiskers) and what shape the data has — symmetric or tailed. That is why the histogram and the box plot are the first thing an analyst draws upon opening a new column of numbers.

definitions.text: bars showing how many values fall into each range (bin). The shape of the data becomes visible: where the hump is, where the tails.
definitions.simple: data sorted into buckets: where it is dense and where it is empty.

definitions.text: the values below which 25% and 75% of the data lie. Between Q1 and Q3 sits the middle half of the values.
definitions.simple: the quarter-marks of the sorted row.

definitions.text: a box from Q1 to Q3 with a median line, whiskers to the edges of ordinary values, and separate dots for outliers.
definitions.simple: five numbers instead of the whole pile: the box is the middle, whiskers are the edges.

definitions.text: the value below which a given share of the data lies: p95 is the threshold below which 95% of values sit.
definitions.simple: what share of the data is below this point.

definitions.text: the formal outlier boundary: a value farther than 1.5 box-widths from the box edges gets flagged as atypical.
definitions.simple: the formal line beyond which a point looks suspicious.

practice: In our pizzeria the salary histogram is a hump around 33–37k plus, once the director arrives, a lonely bar on the right. The box plot lays the same thing out neatly: where the median is, what the middle half looks like (the box) and who sticks out (the outlier dot).

This is the standard first step with any data: before computing means and fitting models, an analyst looks at the shape — it hints whether the data is normal-ish, whether there is skew, and whether outliers might poison the conclusions.
realLife: Histograms are everywhere: the distribution of grades in a class, app load times, audience ages, store receipts. The shape instantly tells you whether the data is "even" or long-tailed.
Box plots are loved in dashboards for comparing groups: put salary boxes by department, or delivery times by city, side by side — and compare center, spread and outliers in one glance.
Shape is also the bridge to the next topic. We kept mentioning the "bell" and the "long tail". Time to see which canonical shapes exist and why.
